title: practical english usage - Part 358
content:
of some quality. The  difference is that they give new information; such Very, great and similar words are also used to talk about a high degree 4 such     and   very,     great,      etc Why did she make such a fuss about the dates?

I'm glad your concert was such a success.

an emphatic descriptive meaning.

Such is also possible with this meaning before a noun alone, when the noun has vocabulary' •  598  suggest ►     467 and suit        fit not I suggest (that) you get. . . (     I suggest I ha I you should gel.) In direct suggestions ('ɪ suggest.. /), should is not generally used.

4 direct     suggestions He suggested that she get a Job in a bank.

He suggests that she get a job in a bank.

Subjunctives (►  232) are also used, especially in American English.

He suggested that she should get a job in a bank.

He suggests that she should get a job in a bank.

Should + infinitive without to is common.

He suggested that she got a job in a bank.

Her uncle suggests that she gets a job in a bank.

Ordinary present and past tenses can be used.

what people should do.

In zhɑr-elauses after suggest, various verb forms are possible when we  suggest 3 verb    forms     in  fAat-clauses not Can you suggest a restaurant to us? (     Can-you-suggest us a restaurant?) Suggest is not normally followed by an indirect object without a preposition.

2 indirect      object     not   used not (     Her unde suggested her to gel a job in a bank.) Iler uncle suggested getting a job in a bank.

Her uncle suggested that she (should) get a Job in a bank.

are common.

Suggest is not followed by object + infinitive. That-cla\ises and -ing structures 1  infinitive       not   used 598  suggest ►     58  4.

For more about the meaning and use of such, ►    596. For more about so, 87.

For so beautiful a day, etc, ►     1 not        so I'm glad you're feeling so much  better, (     . ..     better.) We   use so much,  not so, before comparatives.

We've got so much  to do, and so little time.

We   can also use so before much,  many, few and little.

Why do you talk so slowly?

The milk was so good that we couldn't stop drinking it.

She's so babyish, (     She^s suehbabyish.) We   use so before an adjective alone (without a noun) or an adverb.

2 so   before     adjective,      adverb,     etc Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 599 vocabulary-’  •         suppose, supposing and what if You don't think I'm going to pay for you, surely?

Surely you're not going out in that hat?

’ Tim failed his exam.' ’ Oh,  surely not?

With not, surely can express difficulty in believing something.

’ Is it tonight we're going out? ’ No, tomorrow, surely?

Surely that's Henry over there? I thought he was in Scotland.

’ I'm going to marry Sonia.' ’ Surely she's married already?

These sentences are often like questions.

reasons to believe the opposite, or in spite of suggestions to the contrary.

Surely can be used when we  say that we  think something is true in spite of 2  belief     in  spite    of  . ..

She's surely got the money by now. (‘ It seems very probable.’ ) - I certainly transferred the money on Monday. (7 know.’ ) House prices will surely stop rising soon. (7 believe this must be so.’ ) - House prices are certainly rising fast at the moment. (7 know this is so.') there are good reasons for believing it. Compare: people’ s agreement: to persuade them that something must be true, or that simply tell people that something is true. We   use surely mostly to ask for Surely does not usually mean the same as certainly. We   use certainly when we 1  not   the   same    as  certainly Surely is more common in British than American English.

For more about past tenses with present or future meanings, ►     46.

That was very clever, but supposing you had slipped?

that did not occur.

In sentences about the past, past perfect tenses are used to talk about situations What if I came tomorrow instead of this afternoon?

’ I'm going to climb up there.' ’ No! supposing you slipped!' ’ Daddy, can I watch TV? ’ Suppose you did your homework first.' A  past tense makes the suggestion sound less definite.
